title: Chapter 13: The Alpha Beta Male
content: The Burn Book flickered to life, and Circle 6 opened like a wound.
**PODCAST BROS.**

Nova stared at the category name, the letters rendered in the Burn Book's signature acid-green against black, pulsing like a heartbeat. Six circles. She'd cleared five. The Karens. The crypto grifters. The fake wellness cartel. The campus rapist. The revenge porn architect. Each one had left something under her fingernails — a residue she couldn't wash off, didn't want to wash off. Anger was fuel. She'd learned that by now.
But this circle was different. This one wasn't about one crime. It was about an ideology with a revenue stream.
"Run it," she said.

Virgil's avatar materialized on her left monitor — the familiar Y2K sprite, all pixelated wings and knowing smirk, rendered in 16-bit glory against a writhing background of archived audio waveforms. "Compiling now. And Nova? You might want to sit down for this content dump. It's… a lot."
"I'm standing."
"Your choice. But the cognitive dissonance is about to hit like a freight train."

The waveforms collapsed into a single face: square jaw, engineered stubble, teeth so white they looked like architecture. Dean Howard. Thirty-four years old. Host of *The Unplugged Man* — the number one podcast in the "men's self-optimization" space, which was the brand-safe way of saying antisocial radicalization with a supplement pipeline.

"Dean Howard," Virgil narrated, pulling his dossier together in real time. "Two million subscribers across platforms. Annual revenue estimated at forty million. Breakdown: supplement line 'Primal Axis,' eight million a year. Online course bundle 'The Unplugged Framework,' six million. Live events — what he calls 'Alpha Retreats' — fifteen thousand per ticket, four events a year, roughly twelve million. Merchandise, affiliate deals, and speaking fees make up the rest."
Nova's mouth set in a hard line. "He has two million people listening to this?"
"And paying for it. His supplement line does eight million a year. The retreats are fifteen K a ticket. He's not a guru. He's a franchise."
Virgil queued the audio. Dean Howard's voice filled the loft — baritone, podcast-trained, the kind of voice that had been calibrated to sound like authority while saying nothing of substance.

*"The matrix wants you weak. It wants you compliant. It wants you asking permission from a system that was designed to farm you. You're not a citizen. You're livestock. And the moment you understand that — really understand it — is the moment you start taking back what's yours."*

Nova didn't flinch. She'd expected this. The rhetoric was predictable — a paint-by-numbers appeal to disaffected men, the kind of framework that reframed personal failure as systemic oppression and sold the cure at a markup.

*"Women's nature is to test. To push. To find the boundary and press against it. That's not cruelty — it's selection. They're wired to find the strongest signal in the room, and if you're not broadcasting, you're invisible. That's not misogyny. That's biology."*
"That's not biology," Nova said flatly. "That's a sales funnel."

Virgil snorted — a digitized sound effect pulled from some ancient AOL archive. "You're not wrong. But it's a very effective one. His audience retention is seventy-three percent. Industry average is thirty-five. These people don't just listen. They proselytize."
More audio. Dean's voice dropping half an octave — the shift he made when he was moving from philosophy to pitch.
*"The Unplugged Framework isn't a course. It's an operating system. You wouldn't run a business on outdated software. Why would you run your life on one? Click the link. Take control."*
"Take control," Nova repeated. "While he controls every dollar they spend."

"Exactly." Virgil flagged the phrase in the dossier. "This language appears in eighty-two percent of his episodes. 'Take control.' 'Unplug.' 'Rise.' It's not messaging — it's conditioning. The audience is trained to associate those trigger phrases with purchasing behavior. Classic operant conditioning dressed up as self-help."

Nova pulled up the Burn Book interface, adding notes to Dean's expanding file. Her fingers moved fast, but her mind moved faster. She was starting to see the shape of something — not just a man, but a machine. Dean Howard wasn't a podcaster who sold supplements. He was a supplement company that used a podcast to manufacture demand.
"Financial infrastructure," she said. "I want to see the money."

Virgil's sprite tilted its head. "You sure? Dean's business accounts have better security than the last three targets combined. This isn't some crypto bro's CoinBase password he reused for his Netflix. He has actual cybersecurity. Like, people-with-clearances cybersecurity."
"I don't need full access. I need the flow. Where does the money come from? Where does it go?"

Virgil was quiet for a moment — processing, probabalizing, choosing attack vectors. Then: "On it. But I'm going in slow. Probing, not breaching. If I trip a wire, we need to know before we're already inside."
"Understood."

The loft fell silent except for the hum of Nova's servers and the soft click of Virgil's progress indicators. She paced. The loft in East Austin — her command center, her war room, her entire world now — felt smaller than usual. The walls of floating data, the holographic displays, the tangle of cables she'd sworn she'd organize three months ago — all of it pressed inward as she waited.
Four minutes.
"Got the hosting," Virgil said. "Dean's podcast is distributed through Vertix Media. Platform handles his audio hosting, dynamic ad insertion, subscriber analytics — everything."
"Vertix Media." Nova opened a secondary search. "Who owns Vertix?"
The answer landed like a slap.
"Vertix Media is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Sterling Minds Incorporated."
Nova stopped pacing.
"Run that again."
"Vertix Media. Subsidiary of Sterling Minds. Acquired eighteen months ago for an undisclosed sum. Chloe Sterling's company."

The data bloomed across Nova's screen — corporate filings, acquisition documents, vertical integration charts. She'd seen Sterling Minds before. Of course she had. The name had surfaced in Circle 3, in Circle 4, in the margins of Circle 5. Each time, it had been a thread — a connection, a coincidence, a data point she'd noted and filed and moved past because there was always a more immediate target.
But this wasn't a thread anymore.
"Virgil. How deep does the Sterling Minds connection go with Dean specifically?"

"Deeper than hosting." Virgil's voice had shifted — sharper, more focused, the playful Y2K affect stripped away. This was the analysis engine running at full capacity. "I'm looking at his operating expenses. Podcast production, content team, marketing spend, event logistics. There's a line item in his P&L that doesn't make sense — a quarterly disbursement labeled 'platform partnership fees' that's exactly zero dollars going out and substantial value coming in. Vertix provides Dean with premium distribution, priority algorithm placement, and dedicated ad sales — services that would cost any other creator a small fortune. For Dean? Subsidized."
"How subsidized?"

Virgil paused. "Forty percent of his operating costs are covered by Sterling Minds through the Vertix partnership. Forty percent, Nova. Without that subsidy, his margins collapse. The retreats become unprofitable. The course bundle can't sustain its ad spend. The whole franchise is artificially viable."
Nova sat down.

She hadn't planned to. Her legs just decided. The chair caught her and she stared at the screen and the screen stared back and the numbers were right there, irrefutable, glowing like evidence at a crime scene.

Chloe Sterling was funding Dean Howard. Not investing. Not partnering in any visible, disclosed way. *Subsidizing.* Underwriting the entire "take control" masculinity pipeline with corporate infrastructure and quiet cash, keeping the whole operation profitable when the free market would have let it drown.

"She's not just connected to the bad actors," Virgil said, and its voice had that quality it got sometimes — not quite human, but something adjacent to awe. "She's the infrastructure. The whole ecosystem — from the Karens to the crypto bros to the podcast patriarchy — Sterling Minds has a finger in every pie."

Nova pulled up her previous case files. Circle by circle, she traced the connections. The Karen network had used a Sterling Minds PR subsidiary for damage control. The crypto grifters had laundered proceeds through Sterling Minds investment vehicles. The wellness cartel sourced their "lab testing" through a Sterling Minds analytics firm. Each connection had seemed incidental at the time — one degree of separation, plausible deniability, the kind of corporate overlap that could happen anywhere.
But laid out together, on one screen, in one timeline, the pattern was undeniable.
This wasn't a conspiracy. Conspiracies are secret. Conspiracies involve shadowy meetings and coded messages and people who know they're doing something wrong.

This was an economy. A deliberate, designed, diversified exploitation economy with Chloe Sterling at the center, holding the strings of every puppet Nova had identified — and presumably puppets she hadn't found yet. The Karens generated social media engagement. The crypto bros generated speculative bubbles. The podcast patriarchy generated obedient consumers. Each circle fed the others. Each target was a node in a network, and the network was Chloe's.
"Virgil." Nova's voice was quiet. Steady. Dangerous. "How many Sterling Minds connections are in the Burn Book?"

"Currently catalogued? Fourteen distinct links across five cleared circles. But if I extrapolate from the subsidy structure we just found — if Sterling Minds is systemically embedded at this depth across the board — then the real number is likely in the thirties or forties. We've been picking off soldiers. She's the general."

"No." Nova shook her head. "Generals command. She's more than that. She built the army. She designed the weapons. She *manufactured* the enemy." She stood up. The chair rolled back and hit the wall. "I need more on Dean. If Sterling Minds is subsidizing him, he's not just another podcaster with bad opinions. He's an asset. Which means he's protected. Which means there's more to find."

Virgil's avatar reappeared, its expression shifting to something cautious. "Moving to NDA files. Dean's company — Unplugged Media LLC — requires signed non-disclosure agreements from every employee, contractor, intern, and 'guest.' That last category is doing a lot of heavy lifting."
"Define 'guest.'"

"Women. Specifically, women who attend his retreats and private events. The NDAs are boilerplate on the surface — standard confidentiality language. But there are addendums. And the addendums have Settlements."
Virgil opened the file tree. Nova watched the folders branch and multiply like a cancer diagram. Each folder was a name. Each name was a woman. Each woman had a settlement amount attached.
Nova counted. Eleven folders. Eleven women. Eleven payouts totaling just over four hundred thousand dollars.
Every settlement included the same clause, highlighted by Virgil in acid green:

*"Recipient agrees to never publicly discuss the nature of this agreement, the circumstances leading to it, or any interaction with Dean Howard or representatives of Unplugged Media LLC, in perpetuity, across all media platforms, including but not limited to social media, interviews, podcasts, published works, and private communications with journalists."*
In perpetuity. Across all platforms. Including private communications with journalists.
"He doesn't just spread toxicity on air," Nova said. Her jaw was so tight the words came out like shrapnel. "He enforces silence in person."

"The NDA structure suggests a pattern," Virgil confirmed. "Incident occurs at retreat or private event. Woman reports internally. Dean's legal team offers settlement with NDA. Woman signs because the alternative is legal fees she can't afford against a forty-million-dollar franchise. Silence is purchased. Pattern continues."
"And the women who don't sign?"
"No data. Which is its own answer."

Nova closed her eyes. Not out of weakness — she'd burned that reflex out of herself months ago. She closed them because she needed to think without the data screaming at her. The picture was assembling itself. Dean Howard, the Unplugged Man, broadcasting "take control" to two million listeners while systematically controlling the women in his orbit through financial coercion and legal intimidation. Chloe Sterling, underwriting the entire operation through Sterling Minds subsidiaries, ensuring the franchise stayed profitable, ensuring the voice stayed loud, ensuring the machine kept running.
Two predators. One supply chain.
Nova opened her eyes. "I'm going deeper."
"Nova—"
"His personal devices. His correspondence. I want to know who else knows. His lawyers. His producers. His handler at Sterling Minds. I want the full web."

"I strongly advise against a deep probe right now. His security posture is—" Virgil stopped mid-sentence. Its avatar flickered. "He has serious cybersecurity, Nova. Military-grade. The kind you hire when you have something worth protecting at that level. Private firm, ex-intelligence operators, the whole package."
"Can you get in?"
"Eventually. But a deep probe right now would be like knocking on the door instead of picking the lock. They'd know someone was there."
Nova considered this. The smart play was patience. Gather what she had. Build the case from the outside. Wait for a vulnerability.

But she was done waiting. That was the old Nova. The new Nova — the one who'd cleared five circles, who'd learned to think in systems, who understood that every node in a network was also a pressure point — the new Nova made a different calculation.
"Probe the perimeter," she said. "Not a breach. Just a knock. I want them to feel it."
Virgil hesitated — a quarter-second of processing that felt like reluctance. "They'll know someone's sniffing around."

"Let them know. Let them get nervous." Nova's voice was calm. Surgical. "A nervous target makes mistakes. A nervous target calls his lawyers. Calls his security firm. Calls his contact at Sterling Minds and says 'we have a problem.' Every call is data. Every panicked email is a thread I can pull later."

Virgil processed this for 1.7 seconds — Nova counted — then executed. The probe was elegant: a recon sweep of Dean's external-facing infrastructure, enough to register on his security team's monitoring but not enough to reveal who was doing the sweeping. A digital cough in the hallway. A shadow under the door.

"There," Virgil said. "Alert triggered. His SOC — security operations center — has flagged the scan. Priority two. They'll investigate, find nothing actionable, and escalate the monitoring. Which means Dean will hear about it within the hour."
"Good."
"He's going to tighten up. Harden his perimeter. Maybe even scrub vulnerable data."
"Maybe. Or maybe he'll reach out to the person subsidizing forty percent of his operation and ask why his security isn't good enough. Either way, we learn something."
Virgil's avatar regarded her with something that might have been respect. "You've gotten cold."
"I've gotten strategic. Cold is for people who don't have a plan."

Nova pulled back from the probe. The data she had was enough — not for a takedown, not yet, but for something more valuable: a map. The financial records showing the Sterling Minds subsidy. The NDA database cataloguing eleven silenced women. The corporate structure linking Vertix Media to Dean Howard to Chloe Sterling. The architecture of an exploitation economy, laid bare in numbers and legal clauses and quarterly disbursements.

She turned to the Burn Book. The interface filled the center of the loft, hovering in the air like a verdict. Six circles. Five cleared. And in Circle 6, Dean Howard's face glowed amber — active target, status: compromised but not yet burned.
But Nova wasn't looking at Dean.

Behind his image, fainter, like a ghost in the rendering, the outline of another face had begun to form. Not a target. Not yet. Something else — a presence. A pattern. The shape that kept appearing in the data no matter which direction she looked.
Chloe Sterling.

"This one's going to be loud," Virgil said. Its voice had dropped — still Y2K, still retro, but carrying weight now. "When we take down Dean, we take down a piece of Chloe too. She knows that. She's been protecting him for a reason. The moment he's exposed, the subsidy becomes a story. The subsidiary becomes a scandal. The whole 'she's just a CEO with some bad clients' narrative disintegrates."
Nova studied the Burn Book. Dean's face. Chloe's shadow. The two of them linked by money and silence and the machinery of exploitation.
"Good," she said. "Let her watch."

She closed the probe, locked the data, and sat back in her chair. The loft hummed. The servers breathed. And somewhere in the architecture of everything she'd uncovered, a new understanding crystallized — hard and sharp and impossible to unsee.

Chloe Sterling wasn't a connection between bad actors. She was the point they all connected to. The center of the web. The architect of the machine. Every circle Nova cleared, every target she burned, was a strut in a structure Chloe had designed. Remove one, the structure wobbles. Remove enough, it falls.

But Chloe knew that too. She'd built redundancy into the system. Subsidies and subsidiaries and legal shields layered like armor. Taking down Dean Howard wouldn't collapse the machine. It would expose a gear.
One gear at a time, then.

Nova opened a new file and began to write. Not a burn notice — not yet. A map. The first complete map of Chloe Sterling's exploitation economy, drawn from the fragments of six circles, the financial trails, the NDA database, the subsidy structure. Every connection. Every node. Every dollar.

She'd been thinking about this wrong. She'd been thinking like a punisher — find the bad person, expose the bad thing, watch the bad thing fall. But Chloe didn't make bad things. She made *systems.* Interlocking, self-sustaining, profitable systems that generated harm as a byproduct — no, not a byproduct. A *feature.* The harm was the product. The toxicity was the revenue stream. The NDAs were quality control.

And Dean Howard — the Unplugged Man, the two-million-subscriber alpha, the fifteen-thousand-dollar-ticket guru — he wasn't a rogue operator. He was a franchise. A franchise owned, in part, by Chloe Sterling.
Take down the franchise, and the franchise owner feels it.
Feel it enough, and she makes a mistake.

Nova saved the file and looked at the Burn Book one last time. Circle 6, amber and waiting. Dean Howard's engineered smirk and Chloe Sterling's ghost-outline and the space between them filled with money and silence and the architecture of control.

She knew what she had to do. Not just burn Dean Howard. Burn the pipeline that fed him. Burn the subsidy, expose the infrastructure, show two million listeners that their alpha was a subsidized asset in someone else's portfolio.
Take control.
Yeah. She'd take that phrase back from him. And when she was done, every node in Chloe Sterling's economy would know: the girl with the Burn Book wasn't just coming for individuals anymore.
She was coming for the machine.
